speaker: interviewer
text: It's May the 15th, 1996. The survivor is Vladka Meed Peltel. Renee Firestone, interview. It's in Los Angeles, California, USA. It will be in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's May 15, 1996. I'm speaking to Vladka Meed Peltel. My name is Renee Firestone-- R-E-N-E-E, F-I-R-E-S-T-O-N-E. We are in Los Angeles, California, in the United States, and the language will be English. Good afternoon, Mrs. Meed.

speaker: interviewee
text: Good afternoon.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you please introduce yourself? Tell us your full name, your maiden name? Spell it for us. And tell us where were you born and when?

speaker: interviewee
text: I am Vladka Meed. My maiden name is Peltel. I was born in Warsaw, Poland. And I was the oldest in my family-- mother, father, and three children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you please spell your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: And my name is spelt Vladka-- V-L-A-D-K-A, capital M-E-E-D.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: P-E-L-T-E-L is my maiden name.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your family always live in Warsaw?

speaker: interviewee
text: As far as I remember, my family lived for years in Warsaw. I was born in Warsaw and all my family, my siblings, were born in Warsaw. I even remember the street-- [INAUDIBLE] 3.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Father's name was Shlomo and mother's name was Hanna.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your father's profession?

speaker: interviewee
text: Our family I will describe was lower middle class. My father has a haberdash store. And it was small business. The practical person in the family was the mother. Father was a little bit a dreamer, liked very much to read books. And mother was the one who kept the house and helped out the father in this business too. But we were quite comfortable. Our household was always, as far as I recall, full of people from the family. We didn't have telephone at that time in Warsaw, Poland, so but family used to drop in and all kind of problems to reveal to my father, to my mother. So it was a noisy house, but also an outgoing and of interest what is going on in the family and what is going on with people.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many siblings did you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have a younger sister, two years younger, and a younger brother, four and a half years younger than me.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Henia was my sister and Chiam was my brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Vladka, do you remember what kind of relationship did you have with your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say that I resembled a little bit my father, a little bit in character and also in looks. The relation was quite close. We sometimes used to talk about books and about problems in the world, as a child even. With mother was, as I said, more practical things. Father was also had an attitude that having three children-- and he was very much interested in the so-called social democratic way of life to send one of the child-- of the children-- and I was the oldest one-- to a private school which has also the social democratic attitude in life. So I was sent to a private school and my sister was sent to a public school and my little brother, in the beginning, of course, to the so-called chaider, to the religious school. The relation in our house was very close, a very family close-knitted group of people.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you say that in character you were very much like your father, what do you mean by that?

speaker: interviewee
text: It means that I was in character also interested in things which was going on in the world, not only in my household, and in other countries. Interested in books. I was a passionate reader. And, also, I was outgoing. I love people very much. My father also was outgoing. Always was interested in committees in the house or somewhere else. I remember him taking me to demonstrations when it was the 1st of May as a child, in taking me to lectures. And I was interested in it. My younger sister was more interested in the people of the house, with colleagues, with girlfriends. And my younger brother was so young that it is very difficult for me to say his character, really. A nice, little boy.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the religious background in the family?

speaker: interviewee
text: My background? As being the oldest in the family, I was also looked up that I have to help out when there were difficulties, when there were problems. And in school also I was looked up to perform, to be a little bit an example for the younger. I always want to be a little bit above the children of mine age. In other words, they had expectation of me to be something, a little bit higher than all the siblings. But the relation was very, very close, as I said. And even when I was with the other family people, with an aunt, my mother's sisters, they always, always thought that I will be able to manage or to give advice, being younger. I was quite a good student, I would say always. I was among the first in the class. And later on when-- before the war, when I start high school, my mother, being the practical one, thought that I should also know a trade and she sent me additional to old school to learn how to sew, to learn how to cut forms. And I would say that this maybe saved my life because later when it was formed-- when the German came in to Poland and the walking shops, labor shops, were created, I was able to get into one because of my knowing how to sew, how to cut, how to advise. So, not knowingly, she foresaw that this can be very useful. And it was useful.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you remember your grandparents?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I don't remember my grandparents. They died during the first World War.

speaker: interviewer
text: Both sides?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I don't remember even one grandparent.

speaker: interviewer
text: How about your parents? How were they religon-wise?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father, as I said, he was-- I remember that people used to call him [NON-ENGLISH], which means that he was once upon years I don't remember very religious. But later he turned around and he had more interest what was going on in the world and he became more a social democratic outlook of life he had. So this was the way how he looked at it. And I would say that he was very much involved with what was going on.

speaker: interviewer
text: How about your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mother? Mother was the mother every time and everywhere understanding, the practical person who was thinking about the house, thinking how to bring in food into the house, how to manage things. She was the foundation of the house.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there were no religious customs observed?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was not a religious house, but I would say a very traditional house. When the high holidays came or other holidays, we, like everybody else of our neighbors, like the whole surrounding where we live, was quite Jewish, Yiddish [NON-ENGLISH]. Traditional. And this was also my house. Not the very religious one, but a very traditional one. A Yiddish home. A Yiddish house.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your mother lit candles on Friday night?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Friday night was the Friday Shabbat meal. And at that time, although later on when I was already a member in the children organizations and Friday night we used to get together, I was not permitted to miss the Friday night meal. I have to be at home. This is the tradition which was kept. So it was a Shabbat. But at the same time, it was not very religious. My little brother was sent to chaider to know all kind of religious prayers and so on. But this was like the stop, how far religious it was. But it was a traditional, Jewish home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you experience any antisemitism as a child?

speaker: interviewee
text: The house where we live was also occupied mostly by Jews. It was the Jewish section. It was a so-called [NON-ENGLISH] Jewish ghetto, the Jewish district. Of course, when we used to get out of the Jewish section, very often we had the antisemitic approach if somebody looked Jewish or if somebody behaved a little bit different. But I, personally, didn't experience too much because, as everybody said, that I look not typical Jewish. I look more like a Christian girl, which helped me later on also to survive and to do certain work helping and assist other Jews in danger.

speaker: interviewer
text: As a child what did it mean to you that people didn't look at you as a typical Jew?

speaker: interviewee
text: As a child, it meant more security, to be more able to move around my Polish neighbors. Although we didn't have too close contact with them, but, nevertheless, we were part of the Polish society. And to be able to speak fluent Polish-- and I did speak fluent Polish, although I went to a Yiddish school. I, nevertheless, felt more free being able to mingle among the Poles. And this was, I would say, the most important ingredient of looking as a Polish girl as a child, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you begin to comprehend what the differences were?

speaker: interviewee
text: To comprehend, to understand, and to feel, very early. As long as I remember my childhood, I knew right away that I am Jewish and what certain things to expect from hooligans or from Poles which are antisemitic or they don't like Jews or even to beat them up in front of my eyes. So these kind of experiences I had daily. But, personally, I didn't encounter any specific things. The same thing. My father also didn't look exactly Jewish. But my little brother, my mother, they did look different. I mean, more Semitic outlooks. And very often he came home crying that the boys beat him up because they called him Zyd, Zyd, Zyd. And we had it every days, this kind of incident.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are we talking about the time before Hitler came to power?

speaker: interviewee
text: We are talking the time before the war. Because during the war, when the war started in 1939, things changed. When the war started, the bombardment started over Warsaw, I remember we were in the basement and my father want to quiet us down because we were afraid. We lived on the outskirts at that time of Warsaw, very close to the railroad station, which was constantly an object of bombardment. And he want to calm us down, the kids, and he used to start telling us story that he remembers the German. They are not so terrible. Oh, Hitler talked this. Hitler said that. But he remembers them from World War I. And they were quite pleasant and kind. And you will see that when they will come in, this is not so terrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can I go back for a moment, because we're talking about antisemitism before the war. Did you know or know of antisemitism before, even before Hitler came to power?

speaker: interviewee
text: Before Hitler occupied Warsaw, of course we know.

speaker: interviewer
text: No. Before Hitler became the chancellor of Germany.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in 1933 he became-- yes. Poland is a Catholic land. Antisemitism starts in the church. And the Polish people, and special the simple people, they were constantly-- they have that antisemitism rooted from the childhood. And it was daily occurrences when they said that all you are Zyd or that Zyd was killed Christ. And it was even-- we read in the high schools or in the higher education in the university where Jews were staying not being singled out, not permitted to be the same benches than the other students. And they were-- the pogroms and [NON-ENGLISH] as a child. I remember there other pogroms which were also very characteristic in Poland. Polish in-- the Poles in general were the majority antisemitic, and so it was impossible even as a child not to feel or to have the atmosphere-- to feel the atmosphere about this attitude toward Jews. Before Hitler started his antisemitic propaganda in Germany, but even at that time Poles were the one who picked it up very, very fast. And when my father, for instance, in his haberdash little business he already was faced that Poles was standing before his business and calling not to go in and not to buy in a Jewish stall. So you were facing this kind of thing before Hitler really started the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you explain the fact that Poland was the stronghold of the Jewish culture?

speaker: interviewee
text: I explain, first of all, that the roots are in the Church. I give a lot of-- I blame the church behavior before the war and certainly during the war, and Poles were quite religious. And this is-- I would say, it's rooted antisemitism for generations. Even the history, the Poles. There were kings here and there who permitted or give the same all kind of laws easing up for the Jews. But, in general, the history of Poland was quite antisemitic too.

speaker: interviewer
text: But Jewish culture flourished in this country which was antisemitic.

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish culture-- yes, it was the last hundred years when it flourished and it was a new development of Jewish writers and Jewish literature. This was at the time when the laws were easing up for the Jews. Then it flourished because, in general, the Jews are quite cultural people. And the first opportunity where they could develop a little bit of their own creativity was used at the utmost. It's why it flourished. It was in Poland the largest Jewish population in Europe. Was about three and a half million Jews in Poland. I would say it was about 11 percentage of the Polish population. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So when did you begin to really feel the change?

speaker: interviewee
text: As longer Hitler was settling himself in Germany, as stronger the antisemitism was being felt and seen in Poland. And suddenly with outbreak of the war we felt it more. I mentioned before how my father was assuring us children that the Germans are not so terrible. And the first few days when they came in into Poland, to Warsaw, after the bombardment, after four weeks of really heavy fighting, it was no bread at that time and we have to stay in line. And right the first day when my father went to stay in line for bread, somebody pointed out knowing him that he's a Jew and he was kicked out by a German and beaten up. And this broke him down. He couldn't digest and understand what happened to the German he knew. This was the first encounter right away when they came into Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember that person? Do you remember--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I do remember. I remember because later on I have to take over certain things in our household. A little bit of merchandise which remained from the store which we had we have to start selling out to have something to buy some food. And the only person who could do it was I, looking like a Polish girl. They used to give me the stockings and other things and send me out to the other streets where Poles are living, not where Jews. And my mother and sister was going behind me and looking if a Polish patrol or Germans are not stabbing me, or warning me ahead of time if they are approaching. So, and I was selling the stuff and bringing the money. Later when the order came that we have to put on the armbands, I am used to pull down the armband and go as a Polish girl and sell it. So father was afraid to get out from the house from this first time when he was beaten up. This is why I felt it personally, that I have to be the one who provides for the house some money.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did it affect the rest of the children? How did it affect the other children? Your Sister and your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: It affect everybody. The fact that everybody has to try to help out. They were helping me. And later on, before even the ghetto was created, we start looking for places where we can get some work. I remember my sister was working in a public soup kitchen. I was working later on as a cashier in a small co-operative. And my little brother, he was at that time 10 years old, 11 years old. He was already going to replace his father on labor battalions working. So everybody has to do something, otherwise we would starve to death.

speaker: interviewer
text: Vladka, how old were you when the war started?

speaker: interviewee
text: About 17 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you out of the school already?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was still not finished with high school. I finished my so-called vocational courses, but I was not-- of course, school was right away was cut off. It was not anymore schooling. But at that time we started to look to get into some labor shops and be, first of all, to have some work which is acknowledged by the German, permitted by the German.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the invasion? When the Nazis invaded Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember when they came in. I just mentioned the first time when they started to do line of people for bread. The time when my father was beaten up. Of course I remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened that day in your household?

speaker: interviewee
text: The first thing what happened was that we have to look for food. It was after bombardment and we didn't have any food in the house. And this was the first thing is to look where we can get something. We didn't know to be afraid or not afraid. We were scared, like everybody was scared for the German. But we went out and it was natural that what the Poles are doing we have to follow, just the same way. To go to the places where we can get something, to get the crowds. There were all those day after day, and we have also to be involved in it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And little by little when people with special Jews were coming home and beaten up and when things were being taken away, when all those swear that Jews have to give the fur coat and Jews has to give the jewelry and Jews has to give up their factories and materials and things, so we start feeling and the fear was growing from day to day. What is going to be tomorrow? What new order? Jews are not permitted to go on trains. Jew are not permitted to go into pubs. Jew are not permitted to have stalls. So this, the fear was growing. And this was, I would say, the main atmosphere among Jews, that somehow we are separated. And special when we start having our armbands, we were even more afraid. We were singled out and right away recognized that we are different. How did the Polish population change when these anti-Jewish laws became--

speaker: interviewee
text: The Polish population didn't change. The Polish population was taking this-- of course the Jews were different. They are lower than the Poles. I would not say that they sympathize that we are singled out for the negative way. It was not a big change for them.

speaker: interviewer
text: We will have to stop for a minute. We're going to change tapes.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's a half an hour?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.
